logs.logText:
5/1 Fri — Push

Session time: ~51m

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: 3 continuous reps
: mid-range ROM
Wall Extension 10×2
: normal
Band Dislocate 10×2
: R posterior-shoulder residual was much reduced compared with yesterday
: 2nd set improved
: slight shoulder load remained
Static Position
: Adv. PL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s — no notable issue
Adv. PL PU signal check
: shoulder load present
L Pull-up 5×1
: slightly hard
: required somewhat more effort than usual
WU Notes
: warm-up speed was relatively fast today
: overall condition felt good after daytime nap / recovery
: no meaningful L palm signal carried over from yesterday’s burpee stop

MN
Adv. PL PU 7.7 (7×7) [E1]
S1
: shoulder load was not especially prominent
: main focus shifted toward trunk control
: knee / lower-body position required active adjustment
: static-hold posture and push posture felt slightly different
: working shape was treated as a push-capable bent planche line rather than a copied static shape
S2
: upper-shoulder load became strong, but felt like necessary working load rather than problematic load
: ROM entered fairly deep, similar to old tuck planche push-up feel
: working shape felt close to full bent
: key seemed to be keeping tempo from slowing down
: 7-level success depended more on avoiding drag / stalling than on forcing slow control
S3
: no notable issue
: shoulder load decreased
: slight R upper / posterior-shoulder load appeared in the background
: 7-level accumulation was not yet apparent
: trunk maintenance required a more operational form adjustment
S4
: shoulder load no longer stood out
: accumulated output began to appear
: trunk did not bounce or collapse, but body angle became less clean
: final rep felt slightly hard
S5
: similar to S4
: final rep was relatively hard
: structure still held
: mirror check gave a noticeably fuller upper-body impression, especially around shoulders / upper arms / arms
S6
: accumulated output similar to previous sets
: overall pattern remained stable
: final rep rose with slight trunk rotation
: this appeared to reflect increasing trunk / angle-control cost rather than pure pressing failure
S7
: focused on trunk control
: bent shape remained intact
: volume was recovered at a similar quality level to the previous sets
: full 7.7 volume completed
MN Summary
: Adv. PL PU 7.7 E1 was completed successfully
: this felt close to graduation-ready already at E1
: volume was not a major issue
: bent operating shape remained reproducible across the full 7×7 structure
: shoulder load appeared as normal working load and did not become a limiting signal
: the main limiter was trunk angle / forward-lean control rather than shoulder pain or pressing shortage
: R wrist load appeared from mid-late sets, but remained below signal level
: R wrist / lower-forearm load likely came from controlling forward trunk lean through grip / forearm pressure on parallettes
: rest timer was started quickly with little time loss, suggesting overall output reserve remained
: compared with 6.6 E2, 7.7 E1 felt less like a new challenge and more like confirmation of an already prepared bent-push baseline

CD
HVR 10×1
: from mid reps, shoulder support-zone load felt strong, likely due to push-session residue
: considered switching to ordinary raise range, but continued because it remained tolerable
Wall Extension 10×1
: ROM reduced
: front chain felt locked
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — first rep stiff, then became smoother
: overgrip 6×1 — similar pattern
: little residual signal remained
: shoulder fatigue became more apparent after the session
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

Notes
: today was a strong post-downshift re-entry session
: despite being a 7-level entry session, pace was fast and concentration felt high
: yesterday’s shortened conditioning session may still have provided enough cleanup / reset effect
: no significant carryover from yesterday’s L palm signal appeared during the main push work
: R posterior-shoulder residual was much reduced from the previous day
: main session confirmed that Adv. PL PU 7.7 is already near graduation range, with E2 likely serving mainly as reproducibility confirmation
: cooldown showed front-chain locking and shoulder support fatigue, but no structural restriction or risk signal appeared
: session closed cleanly overall

⸻

5/2 Sat — Pull

Session time: ~54m

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: multiple kick-up attempts early
: performed as 2 / 1 split
: rep 1–2 used shallow-to-mid ROM
: overall vertical push line did not feel especially strong today
Wall Extension 10×2
: ROM slightly reduced
: upper-trap / shoulder load present
Band Dislocate 10×2
: slight R posterior-shoulder residual
: improved slightly on 2nd set
: slight shoulder load remained
Static Position
: Adv. FL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s — no notable issue
: Adv. FL held with slight load
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal to slightly heavy
: intermittent small R shoulder pop at lowest point

MN
A. OAP 7.7 (R7 / L7) ×4 [E2]
S1
: R side passed through dead hang without waiting / resetting
: from rep 6, output load began accumulating
: final rep still maintained ~80% ROM
: L side felt more vertically aligned and stable
: L final rep reduced to ~60% ROM
: both sides showed almost no sway
S2
: R side dropped to a pattern similar to S1 L side
: L side began reducing ROM around reps 4–5
: L completed up to rep 6 with ~30% ROM
: overall heaviness was present, but structure remained stable
S3
: both sides completed rep 1
: then operated around ~50% ROM without fully entering dead hang until about rep 5
: reps 6–7 returned through dead hang and were completed
: upper-ROM / reduced-ROM operation felt more sustainable, but did not feel especially useful due to band rebound
: biceps pumping became more noticeable today than in prior 7-level entry
S4
: both sides used similar reduced-ROM operation
: completed as ~4 short-ROM reps, then dead hang, then 3 additional short-ROM reps
: total volume was recovered
: later reps showed the main grip turning into a hook-like forced hold
: R wrist felt slightly stiff / loaded after S3 onward
: ROM baseline still felt somewhat improved vs E1, but reduced-ROM operation revealed its limit
MN Summary
: A. OAP 7.7 E2 completed full planned volume
: compared with E1, early ROM / alignment seemed improved
: sway was minimal throughout
: R side remained stronger early, while L side again showed better alignment but earlier output reduction
: main cost was not shoulder pain, but bottom/re-ascent demand, biceps/forearm load, and later grip/wrist management
: reduced-ROM operation helped preserve volume, but its training value felt limited when band rebound and hook-like grip compensation became dominant
: today clarified that future OAP progression should preserve meaningful ROM / dead-hang transition rather than relying too much on upper short-ROM volume
: volume was recovered successfully, but this was not a clean graduation-quality session

CD
HVR 10×1
: normal
: later reps produced triceps / long-head load sensation
Wall Extension 10×1
: smoother / more open than warm-up
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — smooth
: overgrip 6×1 — relatively smooth
: residual sensation mostly cleared
: slight shoulder load remained
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

Notes
: session closed cleanly despite earlier OAP heaviness
: cooldown showed improved shoulder state compared with warm-up
: R wrist / grip issue appeared as a late-session support cost, not an acute injury signal
: OAP 7.7 likely still needs additional exposure before graduation
: next session should prioritize meaningful ROM and cleaner pass-through structure over short-ROM volume recovery

⸻

5/3 Sun — Complex Push

Session time: ~1h

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: performed as 2 / 1 split
: mid-range ROM
: balance / continuity not especially strong
Wall Extension 10×2
: ROM slightly reduced
Band Dislocate 10×2
: slight R posterior-shoulder residual
: slightly improved on 2nd set
Static Position
: Adv. PL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s — no notable issue
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal

MN
BAPL45 7.7 (7×7) [E2]
S1
: ring length set slightly longer than usual
: ring / strap hardware contacted the hand repeatedly
: strong side-shoulder signal appeared early
: rhythm split into 4 / 3
: later reps felt more adapted
S2
: 7 continuous rotations completed
: 1st rep felt slightly forced / shallow while finding rotation
: after that, return / rebound rhythm carried the movement
: R side-shoulder load / signal remained after set but did not last long
S3
: felt close to previous session’s peak quality
: after early reps, toes fixed more clearly and lever felt more extended
: during descent rotation, feet repeatedly brushed the rear hanger / bag area
: perceived ROM felt around ~70–80°, possibly just under 90°
: continuous rotation maintained
: shoulder load receded
S4
: similar to S3
: peak-like lever / rhythm quality reproduced
S5
: no major difference from S3–S4
: accumulation not yet severe
: performed at a similar quality level
S6
: slight accumulation appeared
: first rotation felt slightly dull
: after that, rhythm / tempo remained similar
S7
: concentration dropped slightly
: tempo was maintained
: front-back strap swing appeared in pendulum form
: quality was lower than the peak sets
: volume was completed successfully
MN Summary
: BAPL45 7.7 E2 completed full planned volume
: E1’s pendulum-based continuous rotation pattern was reproduced clearly
: S2 already showed full 7-rep continuity, and S3–S5 reproduced peak-like quality
: lever fixation, toe extension, and continuous rotation integrated more clearly than in earlier stages
: actual working ROM felt well beyond a simple 45° category during peak sets, around ~70–80° or possibly near-90° under subjective sensation
: shoulder side-load appeared early, then receded during the main working rhythm
: later fatigue showed mostly as reduced concentration / strap swing, not structural breakdown
: S7 served as controlled volume closure rather than a failed set
: BAPL45 7.7 can be marked as graduated

CD
HVR 10×1
: mid-late reps produced strong shoulder damage / load sensation, especially on R
: from around rep 7, switched down toward ~90–100° raise range
Wall Extension 10×1
: normal
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — stiff / slow
: overgrip 6×1 — R shoulder residual
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

Notes
: BAPL45 7.7 E2 functioned as a graduation-confirming session
: peak rhythm appeared earlier and more repeatedly than E1
: main advancement = stable pendulum rotation + extended lever line under 7-level volume
: longer ring setup was tolerated but created some early hand-contact noise
: late-session shoulder residual appeared mainly in cooldown, not as a main-set limiter
: session closed with full volume completed and no structural restriction
: this room will be closed after this log, with the next 77TR runtime continuing in a new chat starting from the May block

⸻

5/4 Mon — Rest / Delay

Conditioning / Negative + A. Pistol delayed.

5/5 Tue — Conditioning

Session time: ~1h 16m

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: performed as 2 / 1
: mid-ROM
: balance felt relatively good after one rest day
Wall Extension 10×2
: normal
Band Dislocate 10×2
: normal
: R shoulder residual had mostly decreased
Static Position
: Adv. BL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s
: R anterior-shoulder load appeared during Adv. BL
: no notable issue otherwise
L Pull-up 5×1
: R shoulder popped at the bottom position
: slight load + signal appeared
: no pain
: body did not feel heavy or locked overall
: R shoulder still seemed to be adapting / settling

MN
Pistol Negative 5.5 (5×5) [E2]
S1
: R rep 1 achieved a very brief bottom hold, then glute contact
: remaining R reps ended with glute contact
: L side showed a similar pattern
: L final rep reached a brief hold-like bottom position
: opposite knee was intentionally not controlled and stayed bent
: after reps, bottom-hold position was intentionally paused for ~3s to search for the hold feel
S2
: overall pattern similar to S1
: bottom hold every rep was too hard
: forced bottom-hold was performed separately for ~2–3 attempts
: ankle-side control felt insufficient
: ankle effort was clearly high
: R bottom-hold shape itself was difficult to set
: R side needed hand assist to rise / reset
: L side could shift from glute-contact bottom into hold shape with arms extended
S3
: R final rep used forced bottom-hold maintenance attempt
: L final rep was performed with focused natural bottom-hold maintenance
: L side held more naturally than R
: bottom work was concentrated on the final reps rather than every rep
S4
: R side remained similar to previous sets
: L side achieved slight forced bottom hold
: L side could maintain natural bottom hold through 4 reps
: L final rep used slow glute contact, then brief forced bottom hold
S5
: R side remained similar
: L side achieved short natural bottom-hold style reps except rep 1
MN Notes (Pistol Negative)
: Pistol Negative 5.5 E2 volume was completed
: 5-level zone does not need a separate graduation judgment
: next exposure can move to Pistol Negative 6.6 E1
: full-bottom entry was maintained across the session
: R side still showed difficulty assembling the bottom-hold shape
: L side showed clearer natural bottom-hold access, especially from mid-session onward
: ankle / foot-pressure control felt like the main limiting factor
: opposite-knee control was intentionally deprioritized to focus on bottom-hold sensation
: forced bottom hold after glute contact functioned as 1-level bottom-position work
: shoulder pops appeared around the bottom / arm-forward position, likely from pistol bottom posture and arm-scapular counterbalance rather than direct shoulder work

Burpee 40×3
S1 — 1′42″
S2 — 1′29″ / 3′11″ cum.
S3 — 1′29″ / 4′40″ cum.
: S1 pace was intentionally reduced
: focus was placed on palm sensation and posture
: palm-end sensation appeared, similar to the first previous Pistol Negative session
: slight wrist-side sensation also appeared
: signal appeared on both sides, slightly more on R
: intermittent shoulder pops appeared, but similar to previous burpee patterns and not a special issue
: pace dropped during the final ~10 reps of S3 due to clear conditioning fatigue
: feeling of being physically drained by burpee appeared for the first time in a while
MN Notes (Burpee)
: Burpee 40×3 was completed
: support-line signal appeared but did not reach stop-level warning
: palm-end / wrist-side sensation suggests upper-limb support line was still not completely quiet
: signal distribution was bilateral, with slightly stronger R-side sensation
: conditioning fatigue appeared clearly in the final part of S3
: today’s burpee confirmed both recoverable support-line tolerance and return of conditioning load sensation

CD
HVR 10×1
: V-raise was actually good
: later reps felt more abdominal-load dominant
: slight lumbar load appeared
Wall Extension 10×1
: normal
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — slightly stiff
: overgrip 6×1 — relatively smooth, slight shoulder load
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

Notes
: session completed the planned Pistol Negative 5.5 E2 volume
: Pistol Negative can move next to 6.6 E1 without separate 5-level graduation labeling
: full-bottom access remained available
: L side showed clearer natural bottom-hold connection
: R side remained more dependent on forced shape assembly / assist
: ankle / foot-pressure control was the clearest lower-chain limiter
: Burpee 40×3 was completed after intentionally controlled pacing
: palm-end / wrist-side support signal appeared but stayed manageable
: burpee produced a rare clear conditioning-fatigue feeling near the end
: cooldown closed cleanly, with HVR performing better than expected
: today functioned as a successful post-rest conditioning restart and Pistol 5-level zone close.

⸻

5/6 Wed — Push

Session time: ~59m

WU
FR full body
: weak R palm-end / wrist signal appeared under light load
: likely carryover from 5/5 burpee / support-line work
HSPU ×3
: continuous shallow-ROM reps
: no clear wrist signal
Wall Extension 10×2
: normal
Band Dislocate 10×2
: slight R posterior-shoulder residual
: improved on 2nd set
: slight shoulder load
Static Position
: Adv. PL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s
: no notable issue
: shoulder load appeared during Adv. PL
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal
: slight R shoulder load appeared after coming down
: recent R shoulder pop at the L Pull-up bottom position has reappeared after previously quieting down
WU Notes
: body did not feel especially light
: state felt slightly dragged / low-energy after the holiday return and poor sleep
: warm-up lap time was relatively fast at ~11m
: despite low-energy feel, warm-up did not show a stop-level signal

MN
HSPU 7.7 (7×7) [E2]
S1
: structure = 4 / 1 / 2
: kick-up cost was high from the first drop
: set felt somewhat hard
: slight R wrist load appeared
: no pain
S2
: much better than S1
: 6 continuous reps completed
: after rep 6, one forward step appeared
: rep 7 lost concentration / neural attachment and dropped through a weak-ROM negative
: limitation felt more related to neural focus and lap-time accumulation than pure output failure
S3
: similar to S2
: 6 continuous reps completed
: after rep 6, rep 7 dropped through shallow negative descent
: ascent / descent speed and tempo increased slightly
S4
: 7 continuous reps completed
: around rep 4, the line almost escaped forward-diagonal
: held the forward-diagonal bent position for 2s+
: re-fixed the lever and pulled the center back
: recovered balance without coming down
: continued the set and completed all 7 reps
: this was the first clear in-set recovery where a forward escape was held, re-centered, and continued
S5
: rep 1 dropped shallow and came down
: immediately re-kicked up and continued
: additional 7 continuous very-shallow-ROM reps completed
: structure = 1 / 7
S6
: structure = 4 / 1 / 1 / 1
: after the first 4 reps, suddenly walked forward and dropped
: overall wrist / forearm load and pumping sensation increased
: no notable sensation in shoulder or other areas
S7
: shallow-ROM focus
: 6 continuous reps completed
: rep 7 ended with shallow negative drop
: session closed
MN Notes
: HSPU 7.7 E2 volume was completed
: HSPU 7.7 can be marked as graduated
: today did not start from a high-condition state
: before the session, body felt drained with a darker / lower-alert visual feel after the holiday return and poor sleep
: S1 showed high connection cost and slight R wrist load
: from S2 onward, neural attachment and calm arousal rose gradually through the session
: this felt like a controlled arousal switch turning on during the session rather than an excited or forced state
: S2, S3, and S7 repeated a 6-continuous-rep pattern with the 7th rep ending as a shallow negative drop
: S4 produced a meaningful new recovery pattern by holding a forward-diagonal bent position, re-centering, and continuing without coming down
: S5 recovered from an initial shallow drop and immediately completed an additional 7 continuous very-shallow reps
: S6 showed wrist / forearm support-line accumulation, but shoulder and other areas stayed quiet
: compared with 4/26, today may have shown broader repeated 6+ continuity despite lower starting condition
: 4/26 functioned more as a clear 7.7 entry / PR marker, while today functioned more as 7.7 operating stability confirmation
: graduation is supported by the fact that 7.7 was recovered on a non-ideal condition day
: main limiter remained kick-up cost / line attachment / neural focus rather than pressing output
: wrist / forearm pumping appeared late but did not become pain or stop-level signal
: R shoulder remained relatively quiet during the main work

CD
HVR 10×1
: cooldown HVR seems to be gradually adapting
: not especially hard
: later reps produced lumbar / abdominal load
Wall Extension 10×1
: despite anterior-chain use during the main work, it felt smoother than during warm-up
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — normal
: overgrip 6×1 — residual was mostly muted
: only shoulder-load sensation remained
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue
: felt refreshing

Notes
: session completed HSPU 7.7 E2 in ~59m
: 7-level session did not become excessively long
: HSPU 7.7 is graduated
: today’s key value was not peak condition output, but stable 7.7 recovery under a slightly fatigued / low-energy start
: repeated 6+ continuity across multiple sets suggests improved operating stability compared with relying on one peak PR set
: S4’s in-set forward-diagonal recovery was a new and important control marker
: wrist / forearm support-line accumulation appeared in the late sets but stayed manageable
: shoulder did not become a limiting signal during the main session or cooldown
: next Push can transition to PHSPU ROM expansion
: PHSPU should begin as a safe-return ROM mapping phase with foot support reintroduced
: free HSPU should remain in warm-up as floor-ROM exposure / signal-check work.

⸻

5/7 Thu — Pull

Session time: ~53m

WU
FR full body
: fast
HSPU ×3
: performed as 2 / 1
: weak-to-mid ROM
: balance was fairly good despite yesterday’s Push session
Wall Extension 10×2
: ROM slightly reduced
Band Dislocate 10×2
: weak R posterior-shoulder residual
: shoulder load sensation appeared
Static Position
: Adv. FL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s
: no notable issue
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal
: very weak R shoulder pop at the bottom position

MN
Str. FL Row 7.7 (7×7) [E1]
S1
: initial reference hold was maintained for 2–3s+
: tried opening the pelvis while holding
: body felt close to horizontal, with only a small gap remaining
: immediately transitioned into frog-like row
: row tempo was fast
S2
: tried opening the pelvis toward horizontal again
: full horizontal still did not feel available
: reference hold again lasted ~2–3s
: transitioned into row afterward
S3
: reference-hold angle decreased
: hold was kept short
: transitioned into row afterward
S4
: skipped the reference hold
: moved directly into row
: row trajectory started to feel less clear
: volume was completed
S5
: reference hold was skipped
: row felt more stable than S4
: no notable issue
: volume completed
S6
: no trajectory blockage appeared
: later reps felt somewhat hard
: row was pulled more by force in the late reps
: working shape shifted slightly closer to Adv. than Straddle
S7
: later reps showed slightly reduced ROM
: row sensation was maintained
: full volume was recovered
MN Notes
: Str. FL Row 7.7 E1 volume was completed
: early sets allowed near-horizontal pelvic-opening reference, but full horizontal was not fully available yet
: later sets shifted toward compact frog / Adv.-side working shape
: row identity stayed intact without clear progressive trajectory collapse
: rest timer was followed smoothly, and overall session density stayed high

CD
HVR 10×1
: normal
: later reps produced abdominal load
: slight weight / pressure sensation appeared around shoulder + triceps / long-head area
Wall Extension 10×1
: smoother than warm-up
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — slightly stiff
: slight soreness / ache around R posterior shoulder
: overgrip 6×1 — smooth
: slight shoulder fatigue / load sensation
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue
: during Cossack squat passive work with feet planted, pelvis / hips and torso became close to vertical

Notes
: session completed Str. FL Row 7.7 E1 in ~53m, nearly best-record pace for a 7-level session
: despite a sluggish / uncertain pre-session state, execution became stable once started
: R posterior shoulder residual stayed manageable
: cooldown closed cleanly overall
: Cossack passive position showed improved torso uprightness / lower-chain openness.

⸻

5/8 Fri — Complex Pull

Session time: ~43m

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: initial kick-up cost appeared
: 3 continuous mid-ROM reps
: final rep felt slightly hard but was pushed through with strength
Wall Extension 10×2
: normal
Band Dislocate 10×2
: relatively smooth
: residual was low
: improved on 2nd set
Static Position
: Adv. FL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s
: no notable issue
: Adv. L-sit produced slight triceps / long-head load
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal
: later reps were tested with very slow speed to reduce / check R shoulder pop
: pop did not appear except on the final rep

MN
Str. 360 Pull 3.3 (3×3) [E1]
S1
: straddle lever length made the movement feel strongly loaded
: static stop points were less hard than expected
: return timing felt slightly unnatural
: legs had to close / feet had to come together during parts of the return
: knees were not bent
: because the feet came together without knee bend, the lever became closer to a full-length line rather than an easier tuck
: pelvis likely folded slightly to manage the rotation
: back-lever side had limited straddle space due to the doorway pull-up bar ring setup
S2
: accumulated load already started to appear
: return rotation could not be done with the lever kept extended
: especially from the back-lever side, the body had to fold sharply to re-enter the return rotation
: RTO-like elbow load sensation appeared for the first time in a long while
: elbow load felt more like training-use sensation than pain
: load was lighter than early Skin the Cat exposure from the previous stage
S3
: front-side bottom hold also showed much more pelvic fold
: progression felt similar to the Str. FL Row pattern
: even with only 3-level / 3 sets, accumulated load felt enough to drain the line
: back-lever hold also weakened
: return from the back side required much more pelvic fold
: return rotation became closer to STC-like folding
MN Notes
: Str. 360 Pull 3.3 E1 was completed
: next exposure moves to Str. 360 Pull 3.5 E2 under the 3-level exposure logic
: front side had better straddle space and could use Str reference more clearly
: back side was limited by doorway-ring setup, creating narrow / full-like lever moments
: back-side return currently requires STC-like pelvic fold rather than long-lever return
: elbow load appeared as normal training-use sensation, not pain
: lever length is clearly increasing through the STC → Adv. 360 Pull → Str. 360 Pull transition line

CD
HVR 10×1
: normal
: slight abdominal load in later reps
Wall Extension 10×1
: normal
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — normal
: overgrip 6×1 — smoothest in recent sessions
: this level of smoothness felt noticeable after a long time
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

Notes
: session completed Str. 360 Pull 3.3 E1 in ~43m
: this was the first Str. 360 Pull exposure after Adv. 360 Pull graduation
: the main value was first contact with the longer-lever 360 structure and setup constraints
: 3.3 alone produced clear accumulated load
: shoulder line had general use sensation and slight sore-like residual, but no pain
: cooldown closed cleanly, with overgrip band dislocate feeling unusually smooth.

⸻

5/9 Sat — Conditioning

Session time: ~1h 02m

WU
FR full body
HSPU ×3
: kick-up cost appeared, ~4–5 attempts
: 3 continuous reps completed
: from rep 2, straddle was used to stabilize balance
: rep 3 ended with mid-ROM negative drop
Wall Extension 10×2
: normal
Band Dislocate 10×2
: R shoulder residual was clearly weaker
: rotation felt smoother
Static Position
: Adv. BL 6s / Adv. L-sit 6s
: no notable issue
: Adv. BL produced elbow / biceps-side load sensation
L Pull-up 5×1
: normal
: fast ascent / descent
: no R shoulder pop felt

MN
Pistol Negative 6.6 (6×6) [E1]
S1
: R side landed smoothly into glute contact
: bottom hold was mostly unavailable
: R shoulder pop appeared after finishing the bottom position
: pop seemed to occur when releasing from a strongly fixed arms-forward position
: L side achieved short bottom holds on more than half of the reps
S2
: overall pattern similar to S1
: R side achieved one very brief bottom hold
: after that, glute contact became somewhat heavier
: later reps were adjusted back toward softer landing
: L side remained similar to S1
: one knee pop appeared around the middle reps on L side
S3
: R side remained similar
: focus was placed on soft glute contact / controlled landing
: L side remained similar
: final L rep achieved a clear full bottom hold
: holding required the opposite leg to stay actively straight and tense
S4
: R side started landing slightly harder from mid-late reps
: likely due to accumulation
: L side remained similar to previous sets
: from mid reps onward, descent speed was intentionally increased
: tempo was raised
S5
: R shoulder pop continued to appear often after bottom / arms-forward position
: when consciously controlled once in the later reps, the pop did not appear clearly
: R descent speed was intentionally increased
: R glute contact was adjusted to stay soft
: L side showed more visible accumulation from mid-late reps
: L glute contact became slightly harder
S6
: no notable issue
: L final rep finished with a short bottom hold
MN Notes
: Pistol Negative 6.6 E1 was completed
: 6-level volume maintained full-bottom negative access
: R side remained more landing-control dominant than bottom-hold dominant
: L side showed clearer bottom-map prediction and short hold access
: opposite-leg active tension was important for stable bottom hold
: arms-forward fixation likely caused repeated shoulder pops during bottom release

Burpee 40×3
S1
: R side was consciously monitored
: multiple L shoulder pops appeared
: L palm-side load appeared in later reps
: no pain
: sensation was similar to previous palm-support signals
S2
: stopped after ~5 reps
: palm signal became stronger
: pattern felt similar to early exposure-phase support signal
MN Notes
: Burpee was cut early due to palm / upper-limb support warning signal
: signal appeared after repeated contact and strengthened quickly in S2
: limitation was support-contact tolerance, not conditioning fatigue
: next Conditioning can keep Burpee 40×3 in the plan, with S1 used as the diagnostic set
: if palm / wrist signal strengthens in early S2, cut again

CD
HVR 10×1
: normal
: slight abdominal load in later reps
Wall Extension 10×1
: ROM slightly reduced
Band Dislocate
: dorsal 6×1 — normal
: overgrip 6×1 — normal
: one dull internal R shoulder pop appeared around mid reps
: no pain afterward
Passive ROM 6
: no notable issue

NSS-77TR — Monthly Core Summary [2026-05]

May functioned as a transition-closure / NSD-6 re-entry month, not a simple continuation month. Total logged days reached 31, with 27 completed sessions and 4 Rest / Delay entries. The month included multiple previous-stage graduations, full NSD-6 line replacement, new lower-level technical entries, and the first practical STATUS → RECORD transfer test. The main character of May was therefore not just training continuation, but system conversion: completed 7-level lines became the base for new movement maps.

May should be defined as

previous 7-level closure + NSD-6 line transition + new movement mapping + record-system integration.

Overall character of the month

May began by closing several lines from the previous transition block. Adv. PL PU, HSPU, BAPL45, A. OAP, and Str. FL Row all reached closure or graduation-quality markers. After those closures, the system did not continue by simply adding more volume to the same movements. Instead, it shifted into new entries: PHSPU, Str. PL PU, OAP Negative, FL Row, BAPL60, Str. 360 Pull, and Negative + A. Pistol.

The important point is that these new entries began at lower levels without behaving like regressions. The earlier 7-level base carried over as support tolerance, rhythm familiarity, lever control, and signal clarity. May therefore acted as a conversion month where old endpoints became new baselines.

Current working hypothesis

Lower-level re-entry after 7-level closure is not a reset. In the current system, it functions as a mapping phase for longer levers, deeper ROM, new setup constraints, and more specific signal interpretation. Progression quality should be judged by how quickly the new line attaches cleanly, not by whether the visible level number is high.

Rest / Delay entries also remained compatible with the rotation. They did not break the system as long as the next actual completed session resumed from the correct line state.

Primary stage-transition lines

HSPU / PHSPU

HSPU completed 7.7 E2 and can be marked as graduated. The key May change was not a single peak set, but repeated operating stability under non-ideal starting condition. Several sets reproduced 6+ continuity, and one important in-set recovery appeared when a forward-diagonal bent line was held, re-centered, and continued without coming down.

After graduation, the vertical push line transitioned into PHSPU. PHSPU immediately changed the problem from free-balance / floor support into deeper ROM and parallette setup management. Wrist load reduced clearly, while entry / exit cost and face-to-hand ROM became the main practical variables.

Adv. PL PU / Str. PL PU

Adv. PL PU completed 7.7 E2 and graduated cleanly. The 7-level horizontal push no longer behaved like a major event. It felt more like an operating checkpoint, with local shoulder load fading into the background and trunk control remaining the main task.

After that, the line transitioned into Str. PL PU. The new working shape was not yet a clean full straddle line. It appeared as a compact frog / straddle-L-like intermediate push. The main limiter shifted from pressing output to pelvis, hip, and leg-position mapping.

A. OAP / OAP Negative

A. OAP closed at 7.7 E3. It remained one of the hardest lines, but May showed the clearest improvement in output and stamina since A.OAP became the vertical-pull line. Dead-hang pass-through became more available, sway stayed low, and S1 reached the best output so far.

The line then moved into OAP Negative. This confirmed that full one-arm negative is not yet available. The current negative work remains support-dependent, with close-hand start, main-arm loading, hook-like grip tendency, forearm demand, and one-arm dead-hang transition becoming the main information points. OAP Negative now functions as a cleaner range / load-mapping phase rather than a volume target.

Str. FL Row / FL Row

Str. FL Row completed 7.7 E2 and closed the previous horizontal-pull transition block. The endpoint was not a polished full straddle row, but stable frog-like working row plus Str reference access. Row identity remained intact even when late-session ROM reduced.

FL Row then entered better than expected. Removing the straddle split reduced pelvis / hip noise, and the working shape became closer to a bent-knee full-lever-like row. The row trajectory was small but alive. This suggests that the row pattern transferred successfully from Str. FL Row into a cleaner full-line preparation.

BAPL45 / BAPL60

BAPL45 completed 7.7 E2 and graduated through pendulum-based continuous rotation. The important carryover was rhythm, not static strength. Lever fixation, toe extension, and rebound rotation became repeatable under 7-level volume.

BAPL60 then entered as the new Complex Push line. The first exposures corrected the assumption that 80–90° BAPL would be almost impossible. BAPL60 worked through rebound-assisted / pendulum-driven rotation rather than static holding. By 5.5 E1, the line attached cleanly: S1 showed mild entry signal, S2 connected the pendulum rhythm, S3 stabilized lever / rotation synchronization, and S4 reached near-90° rotation through inertia rather than forced output.

Adv. 360 Pull / Str. 360 Pull

Adv. 360 Pull had already graduated at the end of April, and May converted that rhythm base into Str. 360 Pull. The new line immediately exposed setup limits: doorway space, back-lever-side foot contact, limited straddle width, and near-full-like lever moments.

Str. 360 Pull progressed through 3.3, 3.5, and 5.5 exposure. The movement became more familiar, but forearm accumulation and back-side pelvis opening remained the main costs. The next task is smoother pass-through rhythm and cleaner back-lever-side pelvis extension, not static hold polishing.

Conditioning / Lower-chain line

The lower-chain line shifted from Pistol Negative volume toward assisted-ascent mapping. Pistol Negative progressed through 6-level and 7-level exposure, but May clarified that more negative volume was no longer the highest-value target. R side remained landing-control dominant, while L side showed clearer bottom-hold access and early ascent-initiation signal.

Negative + A. Pistol entered after this. Ring-assisted ascent was rejected because it changed the ascent trajectory. Floor-hand-assisted ascent became the current transition method. L side connected better through bottom hold into light hand assist, while R side still needs mapping around hand position, forward body curl, foot pressure, and assisted-ascent path.

Burpee 40×3 stayed in the conditioning slot, but its role became more diagnostic. Palm / wrist support-contact warning appeared earlier in the month, then disappeared after pistol arms-forward adjustment. Later sessions showed R shoulder pop during jump-back transition, with farther-forward hand placement reducing the pop. Conditioning therefore continued as both circulation work and support-contact signal inspection.

Signal / fatigue status

May showed good structural tolerance despite repeated line transitions. Shoulder signals appeared frequently, but most were residual, rhythm-related, setup-related, or post-set traces rather than stop-level warnings. R shoulder residual remained the most common repeated trace, especially around band work, support lines, BAPL rotation, and burpee transitions.

Palm / wrist support-contact warning appeared but did not become a persistent limiter. PHSPU reduced wrist burden through parallettes, while OAP Negative and Str. 360 Pull shifted load toward forearm, grip, and hook-control demand.

Lower-chain signals stayed mostly non-painful. Knee pops appeared around pistol bottom work, but the more important limiter was bottom-hold control, ankle / foot-pressure mapping, and assisted-ascent path. DOMS or ordinary local fatigue did not define the month. Movement quality and signal interpretation mattered more than soreness.

System interpretation

May confirmed that NSD-6 is now functioning as a rotation of transition lines rather than a list of isolated exercises. Earlier main movements are becoming warm-up exposures, signal checks, or base conditions for the next line.

HSPU became the base for PHSPU. Adv. PL PU became the base for Str. PL PU. A. OAP became the base for OAP Negative. Str. FL Row became the base for FL Row. BAPL45 became the base for BAPL60. Adv. 360 Pull became the base for Str. 360 Pull. Pistol Negative became the base for Negative + A. Pistol.

This is the clearest system-level change in May. The month did not simply add more difficulty. It reorganized completed lines into a new operating map.

Monthly conclusion

May was a transition-closure and NSD-6 re-entry month. The month closed several 7-level lines, then converted them into new lower-level technical entries. These lower-level entries did not behave like regressions. They carried over the previous block’s support tolerance, rhythm base, lever awareness, and signal clarity.

Overall, May should be marked as

the month where the previous 7-level transition block closed, NSD-6 became the active operating structure, and new movement lines began mapping from a stronger base rather than from zero.

The next phase should not rush level escalation. The main task is to preserve clean mapping, rhythm attachment, and signal stability while each new line builds its own usable operating pattern.

NSS-77TR — Monthly Core Summary [2026-06]

June functioned as a 6-level expansion / stabilization month, not a simple continuation month. Total logged days reached 30, with 26 completed sessions and 4 Rest / Delay entries. Unlike May, which closed the previous 7-level block and converted old endpoints into new NSD-6 lines, June tested whether those new lines could actually carry higher-volume operation.

The main character of June was therefore not line replacement, but operating confirmation. PHSPU, Str. PL PU, OAP Negative, FL Row, BAPL60, Str. 360 Pull, and the lower-chain Pistol line all moved from initial mapping into clearer volume logic, with several lines entering or stabilizing the 6-level zone.

June should be defined as

6-level expansion + new-line stabilization + strap adaptation + Conditioning restructuring.

Overall character of the month

June began from the May re-entry structure and quickly shifted into 5-level and 6-level confirmation. PHSPU moved from 5.5 into 6.6. Str. PL PU moved from 5.5 into 6.6. OAP Negative moved from 5.5 into 6.6. FL Row entered 5.5 after the early 3.5 exposure. BAPL60 moved from 5.5 into 6.6. Str. 360 Pull became the first current block6.6. Str. 360 Pull became the first current block line to enter 6.6. The lower-chain line moved through A. Pistol / Negative Pistol work and clarified that the current priority is not ascent repetition, but R-side bottom-coordinate access, pre-bottom braking, and soft landing.

The important point is that June did not behave like an unstable jump after May’s transition. Most 6-level entries were recovered without major structural transfer. Some lines showed reduced ROM, folded pelvis, support-hand dependence, or compensation, but these were read as current operating forms rather than failures. June therefore acted as a month where the new NSD-6 map started behaving like a real training structure rather than a fresh experiment.

Current working hypothesis

After a 7-level closure and lower-level re-entry, the next meaningful test is not how quickly the level number rises, but whether the new movement map can tolerate 5-level and 6-level volume without losing signal clarity.

June supports the hypothesis that the current NSD-6 lines are past first-contact mapping and are now entering usable volume stabilization. However, “usable volume” does not yet mean final form completion. In several lines, the current correct standard is still practical rhythm, return safety, support control, and non-transfer of residual signals, not strict visual completion.

Primary stage-transition lines

HSPU / PHSPU

PHSPU became a true 6-level vertical-push line in June. Early 5.5 exposure confirmed that parallette depth, descent path, and return protocol were the main constraints, not wrist burden or general shoulder output. R-side descent pop appeared repeatedly, but it behaved more like a positional / technical artifact than a stop-level signal. Controlled walk-down became more useful than forcing a planche-like descent.

By PHSPU 6.6 E1, total volume expanded to 36 reps. The first three sets stayed stable, S4 marked the beginning of clear output accumulation, S5 added first-ascent cost, and S6 was recovered through ROM management. The main load presented as triceps / upper-arm push-output pumping rather than shoulder or wrist stress.

PHSPU 6.6 E2 then confirmed better late-set stability. ROM was not forced deeper, but it also did not need to be reduced much. S5 maintained concise rhythm and straight lever extension, while S6 stayed nearly similar except for the final rep adjusting to around 70% ROM. This made PHSPU one of June’s cleanest 6-level stabilization markers.

Adv. PL PU / Str. PL PU

Str. PL PU progressed from 5.5 to 6.6, but June clarified that this line is still primarily a pelvis / hip / lower-body mapping problem rather than a pure pressing-output problem. The usable base remained a compact frog-type / straddle-L-like intermediate structure. Full leg extension was not pursued, and ROM depth was not the main criterion.

Str. PL PU 5.5 E1 and E2 confirmed that the compact form could carry 5×5 volume. The main limiter shifted toward pelvis lift, trunk-horizontal maintenance, outer hip / pelvis comfort, and stance width. Shoulder support stayed manageable and did not become a local limiter.

Str. PL PU 6.6 E1 recovered full 6×6 volume, but the session also clarified what should not be done yet. A straddle hold coordinate check showed that the legs can move backward to some degree, but pelvis opening is not yet main-ready. The check added cost, and from S3 onward inner thigh-to-forearm touching / leaning appeared as a compensation pattern. The next phase should avoid additional straddle hold checks and stabilize the compact frog-type base first.

A. OAP / OAP Negative

OAP Negative remained the highest-density Pull line in June. The line progressed from 5.5 into 6.6, but full one-arm negative was still not the target. Support-dependent control remained the correct working form.

OAP Negative 5.5 E1 confirmed partial release capacity in early reps, with only one or two support fingers remaining at times, but grip / forearm fatigue quickly became the main limiter. L forearm fatigue was stronger than R. By E2, support range decreased, especially on L, and a more compact L descent tempo improved forearm management.

OAP Negative 6.6 E1 introduced an almost-center support grip. This became the likely baseline going forward. The center-grip setup is important because it creates a bridge toward future partial release or hand-release work, but no release was attempted in June’s 6.6 exposure. The main lesson was that L-side limitation is strongly tied to braking-time cost, not only raw output. OAP Negative therefore remains a bridge line toward full-negative re-entry, not an ascent line yet.

Str. FL Row / FL Row

FL Row became clearer in June. The working shape remained a bent-knee full-lever-like row rather than a frog row, but June also clarified that visual lever length should not override row trajectory.

FL Row 3.5 E2 confirmed that the movement was clearly outside the previous frog-like row pattern. The thighs stayed more extended, pelvis folding was reduced, and shallow up / down row trajectory remained visible. This made the line ready for 5.5.

FL Row 5.5 E1 then showed a range of working forms. S1–S2 used a longer bent-lever shape, including a near-full-lever hold check before the first rep. From S3 onward, the knees gradually moved closer to the torso and the pelvis became more vertical. ROM became shallow / pecking-like later, but row trajectory remained visible.

FL Row 5.5 E2 confirmed that the practical current target is not deeper ROM or longer lever display. The priority is keeping a visible row trajectory inside the usable bent-lever shape. Moving the knees farther forward lengthened the shape but reduced row quality, while bringing them slightly back restored practical row mechanics.

BAPL45 / BAPL60

BAPL60 became one of the strongest lines in June. The line moved from 5.5 E2 into 6.6 E1 and E2 without rhythm collapse. Its operating identity is now clearly fast pendulum-driven forward rotation, not static holding or slow-control grinding.

BAPL60 5.5 E2 reproduced the May pendulum pattern, including near 80–90° rotation with lever stability. Shoulder-side and axilla-inner-line sensations appeared as use-sensations rather than bad signals.

BAPL60 6.6 E1 confirmed that the line had already moved beyond the literal 60-title angle. Rotation approached roughly 80–90° once rhythm attached, and further angle expansion was judged unnecessary. Intentional slowing at the lowest horizontal return point confirmed that control exists, but it increased shoulder-side load. Therefore the future standard should remain fast pendulum rhythm, lever stability, full-volume recovery, and signal non-transfer.

BAPL60 6.6 E2 strengthened this interpretation. S3 clearly showed a Viking / pendulum-like inertia rhythm, where rotation felt driven by attached momentum rather than active force. S4–S5 had no time loss or stamina drop, and S6 closed with only R side-posterior shoulder usage sensation. BAPL60 now looks close to title-level completion logic, with the next step depending on whether the system treats 60 as a graduation point or uses one more stabilization pass.

Adv. 360 Pull / Str. 360 Pull

Str. 360 Pull became the first current block line to enter 6.6 in June. The key improvement was not stricter lever shape, but better pass-through rhythm and lower forearm cost.

At 5.5 E2, June confirmed that slow stop-point holding increased forearm load. Faster controlled pass-through reduced braking cost. Back-lever-side shape remained partly setup-limited due to doorway space, and late-set fatigue appeared as lumbar / pelvis folding.

Str. 360 Pull 6.6 E1 expanded from 25 reps to 36 reps. Despite the volume increase, forearm load was noticeably lower than the previous exposure. This likely came from better rhythm, faster pass-through speed, and improved holding-output endurance. S3 showed that ring / strap forward-back swing was a main instability point, while S4–S5 recovered rhythm. S6 still showed significant pelvis / lumbar folding, especially after the back-lever-side transition.

Str. 360 Pull 6.6 E2 was treated as a recovery-focused E2 session rather than posture improvement. Pelvis folding was allowed, strict horizontal stop points were not forced, and full volume was recovered. This confirms that current Str. 360 Pull should prioritize efficient pass-through rhythm under the doorway setup, not strict straight-line lever display.

Conditioning / Lower-chain line

June made the clearest Conditioning restructure so far. The month began with Negative + A. Pistol and Burpee still sharing the Conditioning slot, but by the end of the month Burpee had been officially removed from the fixed post-Pistol structure.

The biggest early marker was 6/1, where L-side full ascent first appeared. L ascent moved from arm-swing counterbalance into arms-forward control in the same session. However, R remained behind, with bottom hold unavailable and controlled glute contact still the main pattern.

From 5.5 onward, the system clarified that R assisted ascent was premature. R needed bottom-coordinate / balance-map access rather than ascent output. Bottom-start micro-hold exploration helped identify the missing layer. In 6.6 E1, R produced a brief 0.x-second bottom-hold signal, but it did not remain stable under fatigue. Later exposures shifted away from “hold success” and toward pre-bottom braking, soft glute contact, and repeated micro-hold access.

The Burpee decision was the major system-level change. Early June Burpee sessions stayed useful and even showed strong output after Pistol work. But by 6/19 and 6/23, post-Pistol Burpee created anticipatory burden, focus drop, and low-arousal shutdown risk. On 6/29, Burpee was officially excluded from the structure from the start, and Negative Pistol 7.7 E2 recovered full 7×7 volume with less mental and physical burden. R produced repeated pre-bottom braking / micro-hold signals, while L stayed as the stronger but non-standard running-mate side.

Signal / fatigue status

June showed high structural tolerance under 6-level expansion. Shoulder signals were frequent, but they mostly behaved as WU / strap / band / trajectory-specific residuals rather than main-work injury signals. The R shoulder line remained the main repeated signal, especially around band / strap work, PHSPU descent, BAPL60 rotation, and occasional Pistol-related catches.

The non-elastic strap became one of the clearest secondary adaptation variables. It arrived around mid-month and initially produced stronger transition-gate aching. Unlike the previous elastic band, the strap felt more like a short, firm rotation gate than a large-arc tension stretch. Early sessions showed WU aching and CD smoothing. By late June, aching had clearly decreased, dorsal / overgrip rotation felt smoother, and L Pull-up bottom-position R shoulder pop appeared reduced or absent in several exposures.

Forearm / grip load became more important than shoulder load in the Pull lines. OAP Negative emphasized L forearm braking-time cost, while Str. 360 Pull showed that slow stop-point holding increases forearm demand. FL Row was less forearm-dominant and instead clarified lever-shape vs row-trajectory tradeoff.

Lower-chain signals stayed mostly non-painful but technically important. R Pistol remained bottom-coordinate dominant. L side was stronger and showed ascent / hold ability, but accumulated output and knee-pop signals meant it should stay as a running-mate, not the standard. Conditioning fatigue became more about focus, arousal, and total session structure than pure lower-body failure.

System interpretation

May confirmed that NSD-6 had become the active operating map. June confirmed that this map can carry 5-level and 6-level expansion.

The clearest system-level change in June is that several lines now have their own operating criteria:

PHSPU is judged by return safety, descent path, triceps-output recovery, and non-transfer of shoulder / wrist signals.

Str. PL PU is judged by compact frog-type support, pelvis lift, trunk-horizontal control, and reduction of thigh-to-forearm compensation.

OAP Negative is judged by support-dependent negative control, center grip, L braking-time management, and no premature full release.

FL Row is judged by visible shallow row trajectory within a usable bent-lever shape, not strict full-lever display.

BAPL60 is judged by fast pendulum rhythm, lever stability, full-volume recovery, and shoulder-signal absorption into rhythm.

Str. 360 Pull is judged by efficient pass-through rhythm and forearm management, not strict stop-point holding.

Conditioning is now judged by Pistol-only lower-chain mapping, especially R pre-bottom braking / micro-hold access, with Burpee removed from the fixed post-Pistol structure.

This is the main June conversion. The system no longer treats each exercise as a generic level target. Each line now has a specific internal success criterion.

Monthly conclusion

June was a 6-level expansion and stabilization month. The month did not replace the NSD-6 lines created in May. Instead, it tested whether those new lines could hold volume, rhythm, and signal clarity under increased level demand.

Overall, June should be marked as

the month where NSD-6 moved from new-line mapping into usable 6-level operation, while Conditioning was restructured around Pistol-only priority and strap-related residual signals began adapting rather than escalating.

The next phase should avoid rushing visual form completion. The main task is to preserve each line’s current success criterion: PHSPU return safety, Str. PL compact structure, OAP center-grip negative control, FL Row trajectory, BAPL60 pendulum rhythm, Str. 360 pass-through efficiency, and Pistol R-side pre-bottom braking.

July should continue from this 6-level stabilization base, with progression allowed only where live signal remains clean and residual signals continue to stay non-transferable.
